questions.question: Reverse a string in C# — multiple approaches
questions.answer:
// Method 1: LINQ (cleanest for interviews)
string Reverse1(string s) =>
    new string(s.Reverse().ToArray());

// Method 2: Array.Reverse (most readable)
string Reverse2(string s)
{
    var chars = s.ToCharArray();
    Array.Reverse(chars);
    return new string(chars);
}

// Method 3: StringBuilder (manual, shows understanding)
string Reverse3(string s)
{
    var sb = new StringBuilder(s.Length);
    for (int i = s.Length - 1; i >= 0; i--)
        sb.Append(s[i]);
    return sb.ToString();
}

// Method 4: Two-pointer in-place on char array
string Reverse4(string s)
{
    var chars = s.ToCharArray();
    int left = 0, right = chars.Length - 1;
    while (left < right)
    {
        (chars[left], chars[right]) = (chars[right], chars[left]);
        left++; right--;
    }
    return new string(chars);
}

// Edge cases to mention:
// - null/empty string → return as-is
// - Unicode surrogate pairs (emoji) break char-level reversal
// - For Unicode safety: use StringInfo.GetTextElementEnumerator()

Time: O(n) for all methods. Space: O(n) for all (strings are immutable in C#, always need a new string).
followUps: How do you reverse a string without using built-in methods?
How do you reverse words in a sentence?
What about Unicode/emoji characters?

followUpAnswers.How do you reverse a string without usin:
Reversing a string without built-in helpers means manually swapping characters using a two-pointer approach on a char[]. Since strings in C# are immutable, you always convert to a character array first, perform the swap, then construct a new string.

string ReverseManual(string s)
{
    if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(s)) return s;
    char[] chars = new char[s.Length];
    // Copy characters in reverse order
    for (int i = 0; i < s.Length; i++)
        chars[i] = s[s.Length - 1 - i];
    return new string(chars);
}

// Two-pointer swap variant (in-place on the char array)
string ReverseTwoPointer(string s)
{
    if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(s)) return s;
    char[] chars = s.ToCharArray();
    int left = 0, right = chars.Length - 1;
    while (left < right)
    {
        // Manual XOR swap (no temp variable)
        chars[left] ^= chars[right];
        chars[right] ^= chars[left];
        chars[left] ^= chars[right];
        left++;
        right--;
    }
    return new string(chars);
}

The two-pointer swap is O(n) time and O(n) space (for the char array). The XOR trick avoids a temporary variable but is considered less readable — use a simple temp swap in interviews unless specifically asked. Always handle null/empty edge cases and mention that the result is a new string, not an in-place mutation of the original.
followUpAnswers.How do you reverse words in a sentence?:
Reversing words in a sentence means splitting on whitespace, reversing the array of words, and joining them back. The key edge cases are multiple consecutive spaces and leading/trailing whitespace.

// Simple approach — split, reverse array, join
string ReverseWords(string sentence)
{
    if (string.IsNullOrWhiteSpace(sentence)) return sentence;
    string[] words = sentence.Split(' ', StringSplitOptions.RemoveEmptyEntries);
    Array.Reverse(words);
    return string.Join(' ', words);
}

// Manual approach without Split (shows algorithm knowledge)
string ReverseWordsManual(string s)
{
    char[] chars = s.Trim().ToCharArray();
    // Step 1: reverse the entire string
    Reverse(chars, 0, chars.Length - 1);
    // Step 2: reverse each word individually
    int start = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i <= chars.Length; i++)
    {
        if (i == chars.Length || chars[i] == ' ')
        {
            Reverse(chars, start, i - 1);
            start = i + 1;
        }
    }
    return new string(chars);
}

void Reverse(char[] chars, int l, int r)
{
    while (l < r) { (chars[l], chars[r]) = (chars[r], chars[l]); l++; r--; }
}

// Example:
// Input:  "Hello World .NET"
// Output: ".NET World Hello"

The two-step manual approach (reverse entire string, then reverse each word) is an elegant O(n) in-place algorithm. StringSplitOptions.RemoveEmptyEntries handles multiple spaces correctly. Always clarify with the interviewer whether punctuation should be treated as part of a word.
followUpAnswers.What about Unicode/emoji characters?:
C# strings are sequences of UTF-16 code units (char values). Many Unicode characters outside the Basic Multilingual Plane — including most emoji — require two char values called a surrogate pair. Naively reversing at the char level splits these pairs and produces garbled or invalid output.

// WRONG — breaks emoji/surrogate pairs
string bad = new string("Hello 🎉".Reverse().ToArray());
// Result is corrupted: the emoji becomes two swapped surrogates

// CORRECT — use StringInfo to enumerate text elements
using System.Globalization;

string ReverseUnicodeSafe(string s)
{
    var elements = new List();
    var enumerator = StringInfo.GetTextElementEnumerator(s);
    while (enumerator.MoveNext())
        elements.Add(enumerator.GetTextElement());
    elements.Reverse();
    return string.Concat(elements);
}

// Example:
Console.WriteLine(ReverseUnicodeSafe("Hello 🎉")); // "🎉 olleH"
Console.WriteLine(ReverseUnicodeSafe("café"));     // "éfac" (correct)

// .NET 6+ alternative using EnumerateRunes
string ReverseRunes(string s)
{
    var runes = s.EnumerateRunes().ToArray();
    Array.Reverse(runes);
    return string.Concat(runes.Select(r => r.ToString()));
}

StringInfo.GetTextElementEnumerator handles surrogate pairs and combining characters (e.g., accented letters formed by a base character + combining diacritic). EnumerateRunes() (available from .NET 5+) handles surrogate pairs but not grapheme clusters with combining characters — StringInfo is the safest choice when full Unicode correctness is needed. Mentioning this trade-off demonstrates senior-level awareness in an interview.

questions.tip: Interview answer: Show Method 1 (LINQ) as the production approach, then Method 4 (two-pointer) to show you understand the algorithm. Mention the Unicode edge case — it shows senior-level thinking.

questions.question: Check if a string is a palindrome
questions.answer:
// Method 1: Two-pointer (most efficient)
bool IsPalindrome(string s)
{
    int left = 0, right = s.Length - 1;
    while (left < right)
    {
        if (s[left] != s[right]) return false;
        left++;
        right--;
    }
    return true;
}

// Method 2: Compare with reversed (simple but creates extra string)
bool IsPalindrome2(string s) =>
    s == new string(s.Reverse().ToArray());

// Variant: ignore case, non-alphanumeric (common follow-up)
bool IsPalindromeAlphanumeric(string s)
{
    var clean = s.Where(char.IsLetterOrDigit)
                 .Select(char.ToLower)
                 .ToArray();
    int l = 0, r = clean.Length - 1;
    while (l < r)
    {
        if (clean[l] != clean[r]) return false;
        l++; r--;
    }
    return true;
}

// Examples:
// IsPalindrome("racecar") → true
// IsPalindrome("hello") → false
// IsPalindromeAlphanumeric("A man a plan a canal Panama") → true

Time: O(n). Space: O(1) for two-pointer (O(n) if you create the reversed string).
followUps: What is the longest palindromic substring?
How do you check if a number is a palindrome?

followUpAnswers.What is the longest palindromic substrin:
The Longest Palindromic Substring problem asks you to find the longest contiguous substring of a string that is itself a palindrome. The classic O(n²) solution uses expand-around-center: for each position in the string, try to expand outward as long as the characters match, tracking the longest palindrome found.

// Expand-around-center — O(n²) time, O(1) space
string LongestPalindromicSubstring(string s)
{
    if (string.IsNullOrEmpty(s)) return s;
    int start = 0, maxLen = 1;

    void Expand(int left, int right)
    {
        while (left >= 0 && right < s.Length && s[left] == s[right])
        {
            if (right - left + 1 > maxLen)
            {
                maxLen = right - left + 1;
                start  = left;
            }
            left--;
            right++;
        }
    }

    for (int i = 0; i < s.Length; i++)
    {
        Expand(i, i);     // odd-length palindromes (center = single char)
        Expand(i, i + 1); // even-length palindromes (center = between chars)
    }
    return s.Substring(start, maxLen);
}

// Examples:
// "babad" → "bab" (or "aba")
// "cbbd"  → "bb"
// "racecar" → "racecar"

There is also Manacher's Algorithm that solves this in O(n) time by reusing previously computed palindrome radii, but it is complex and rarely expected in standard interviews. The expand-around-center approach is the sweet spot — demonstrate it and mention that Manacher's exists as an O(n) optimization if pressed.

A Dynamic Programming table approach (fill a 2D boolean table dp[i][j]) also works in O(n²) time but requires O(n²) space, making it worse than the expand approach on both dimensions in practice.
followUpAnswers.How do you check if a number is a palind:
There are two approaches: string conversion (simple) and mathematical digit reversal (no extra memory, preferred for follow-ups). Negative numbers are never palindromes. Numbers ending in 0 (except 0 itself) are also never palindromes, since the leading digit of the reversed number would be 0.

// Approach 1: Convert to string and use two-pointer
bool IsNumberPalindrome(int n)
{
    if (n < 0) return false;  // negative numbers are never palindromes
    string s = n.ToString();
    int left = 0, right = s.Length - 1;
    while (left < right)
    {
        if (s[left] != s[right]) return false;
        left++; right--;
    }
    return true;
}

// Approach 2: Mathematical reversal (no string allocation)
bool IsNumberPalindromeMath(int n)
{
    if (n < 0) return false;
    if (n != 0 && n % 10 == 0) return false; // e.g., 10, 100 → not palindromes

    int original = n;
    int reversed = 0;
    while (n > 0)
    {
        reversed = reversed * 10 + n % 10;  // extract last digit, append to reversed
        n /= 10;
    }
    return original == reversed;
}

// Optimised: only reverse half the number (avoids overflow risk)
bool IsNumberPalindromeHalf(int n)
{
    if (n < 0 || (n != 0 && n % 10 == 0)) return false;
    int reversed = 0;
    while (n > reversed)  // stop when we've processed half
    {
        reversed = reversed * 10 + n % 10;
        n /= 10;
    }
    // For even-length: n == reversed
    // For odd-length: n == reversed / 10 (middle digit doesn't matter)
    return n == reversed || n == reversed / 10;
}

// Examples:
// 121  → true
// -121 → false
// 1221 → true
// 10   → false

The half-reversal trick is the most elegant solution: it avoids building the full reversed number (which could theoretically overflow for very large inputs) and stops processing at the midpoint. Interviewers often ask for this optimization after you give the basic solution.

questions.tip: For numbers: Convert to string and use the same approach, OR reverse mathematically (no string conversion): extract digits via modulo and rebuild in reverse. Mention both.

questions.question: Find the factorial of a number — recursive and iterative
questions.answer:
// Iterative (preferred — no stack overflow risk)
long Factorial(int n)
{
    if (n < 0) throw new ArgumentException("n must be non-negative");
    long result = 1;
    for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++)
        result *= i;
    return result;
}

// Recursive (elegant but stack overflow risk for large n)
long FactorialRecursive(int n)
{
    if (n < 0) throw new ArgumentException("n must be non-negative");
    if (n <= 1) return 1;  // base case
    return n * FactorialRecursive(n - 1);
}

// With memoization (if calling frequently with same values)
private static readonly Dictionary<int, long> _cache = new();
long FactorialMemo(int n)
{
    if (n <= 1) return 1;
    if (_cache.TryGetValue(n, out var cached)) return cached;
    var result = n * FactorialMemo(n - 1);
    _cache[n] = result;
    return result;
}

// Note: long overflows at 21! (use BigInteger for large n)
// using System.Numerics;
BigInteger FactorialBig(int n)
{
    BigInteger result = 1;
    for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++) result *= i;
    return result;
}
followUps: What's the maximum factorial that fits in a long?
How would you compute factorial of very large numbers?

followUpAnswers.What's the maximum factorial that fits i:
20! is the largest factorial that fits in a long (Int64). Its value is 2,432,902,008,176,640,000, which is just within the long.MaxValue of 9,223,372,036,854,775,807. 21! = 51,090,942,171,709,440,000 which overflows.

nn! valueFits in long?
186,402,373,705,728,000Yes
19121,645,100,408,832,000Yes
202,432,902,008,176,640,000Yes (max)
2151,090,942,171,709,440,000No — overflows
// Detect overflow with checked arithmetic
long FactorialChecked(int n)
{
    long result = 1;
    try
    {
        for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++)
            result = checked(result * i);  // throws OverflowException if too big
    }
    catch (OverflowException)
    {
        throw new ArgumentOutOfRangeException(nameof(n),
            $"n={n} causes overflow. Max is 20 for long.");
    }
    return result;
}

Console.WriteLine(long.MaxValue);  // 9,223,372,036,854,775,807
Console.WriteLine(Factorial(20));  // 2,432,902,008,176,640,000  ✓
// Factorial(21) → OverflowException

Using the checked keyword forces the CLR to throw an OverflowException on integer overflow instead of silently wrapping, which is important for correctness. In performance-critical inner loops you'd use unchecked (the default), but for financial or scientific calculations always prefer checked.
followUpAnswers.How would you compute factorial of very:
For factorials beyond 20!, you need arbitrary-precision integers. .NET ships System.Numerics.BigInteger which can represent integers of any size, limited only by available memory. The performance degrades because BigInteger arithmetic is O(d²) where d is the number of digits.

using System.Numerics;

// BigInteger factorial — handles any non-negative integer
BigInteger FactorialBig(int n)
{
    if (n < 0) throw new ArgumentException("n must be >= 0");
    BigInteger result = BigInteger.One;
    for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++)
        result *= i;
    return result;
}

// Example outputs:
// 25!  = 15,511,210,043,330,985,984,000,000
// 50!  = 30,414,093,201,713,378,043,612,608,166,979,581,188,299,763,898,377,856,000,000,000,000
// 100! = (158 digits)

Console.WriteLine(FactorialBig(100).ToString().Length); // 158 digits

// Parallel/divide-and-conquer for very large n (e.g., 10000!)
// Split: n! = (1 * 2 * ... * n/2) * ((n/2+1) * ... * n)
// Each half can be computed independently
BigInteger FactorialParallel(int n)
{
    if (n <= 1) return BigInteger.One;
    int mid = n / 2;
    BigInteger left = BigInteger.One, right = BigInteger.One;
    Parallel.Invoke(
        () => { for (int i = 2; i <= mid; i++) left *= i; },
        () => { for (int i = mid + 1; i <= n; i++) right *= i; }
    );
    return left * right;
}

For extremely large factorials (thousands of digits), more sophisticated algorithms exist: prime factorization approach (compute the prime factorization of n! using Legendre's formula, then multiply) and binary splitting are O(n (log n)²) but are research-level optimizations. For interviews, demonstrating BigInteger with a note that parallelism can be applied to ranges is the ideal answer level.

questions.tip: 20! = 2,432,902,008,176,640,000 (fits in long). 21! overflows long. Mention this — it shows awareness of integer overflow, a common production bug.

questions.question: Find the Fibonacci sequence — iterative, recursive, and dynamic programming
questions.answer:
// Naive recursive — O(2^n), terrible for large n
long FibRecursive(int n)
{
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    return FibRecursive(n - 1) + FibRecursive(n - 2);
}

// Iterative — O(n) time, O(1) space (best for interviews)
long FibIterative(int n)
{
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    long prev = 0, curr = 1;
    for (int i = 2; i <= n; i++)
    {
        long next = prev + curr;
        prev = curr;
        curr = next;
    }
    return curr;
}

// Memoization — O(n) time, O(n) space
long FibMemo(int n, Dictionary<int, long>? memo = null)
{
    memo ??= new Dictionary<int, long>();
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    if (memo.TryGetValue(n, out var cached)) return cached;
    var result = FibMemo(n - 1, memo) + FibMemo(n - 2, memo);
    memo[n] = result;
    return result;
}

// Using IEnumerable to stream the sequence
IEnumerable<long> FibSequence()
{
    long prev = 0, curr = 1;
    yield return prev;
    yield return curr;
    while (true)
    {
        long next = prev + curr;
        yield return next;
        prev = curr;
        curr = next;
    }
}
followUps: What is the time complexity of each approach?
How do you get the first N Fibonacci numbers?

followUpAnswers.What is the time complexity of each appr:
Each Fibonacci implementation has dramatically different performance characteristics. Understanding why is as important as knowing the numbers.

ApproachTime ComplexitySpace ComplexityNotes
Naive RecursiveO(2n)O(n) call stackRecomputes same subproblems exponentially
Memoized RecursiveO(n)O(n)Each F(k) computed once, cached in dictionary
Bottom-up DP (array)O(n)O(n)Fills dp[] from 0..n; avoids recursion overhead
Iterative (2 vars)O(n)O(1)Best for single value; no extra memory
Matrix ExponentiationO(log n)O(log n) stackUses fast matrix power; rarely needed in interviews
// Why naive recursive is O(2^n) — the recursion tree:
// F(5)
// ├── F(4)
// │   ├── F(3)
// │   │   ├── F(2) ← computed again!
// │   │   └── F(1)
// │   └── F(2)     ← computed again!
// └── F(3)          ← computed again!
//     ├── F(2)
//     └── F(1)
// Each level doubles the number of calls → O(2^n)

// Matrix exponentiation for O(log n):
// [F(n+1)] = [1 1]^n * [1]
// [F(n)  ]   [1 0]     [0]
long FibMatrix(int n)
{
    if (n <= 1) return n;
    long[,] result = MatPow(new long[,] { { 1, 1 }, { 1, 0 } }, n);
    return result[0, 1];
}

For interviews, stating O(2^n) for naive and O(n)/O(1) for iterative is sufficient. Mentioning the O(log n) matrix exponentiation approach demonstrates deeper knowledge and is a great closer when asked "can we do better than O(n)?"
followUpAnswers.How do you get the first N Fibonacci num:
There are several ways to generate the first N Fibonacci numbers. The cleanest production approach uses a generator (yield return) or builds a list iteratively. Avoid the naive recursive approach since calling F(0)..F(N-1) recursively would be O(N·2^N).

// Method 1: Build a list iteratively (simple, recommended)
List FirstNFibs(int n)
{
    if (n <= 0) return new List();
    var result = new List(n);
    long prev = 0, curr = 1;
    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
    {
        result.Add(prev);
        (prev, curr) = (curr, prev + curr);
    }
    return result;
}
// FirstNFibs(8) → [0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, 8, 13]

// Method 2: Infinite generator — take N lazily
IEnumerable FibSequence()
{
    long prev = 0, curr = 1;
    while (true)
    {
        yield return prev;
        (prev, curr) = (curr, prev + curr);
    }
}

// Usage: take first 10
var first10 = FibSequence().Take(10).ToList();

// Method 3: Using LINQ Aggregate with seed state (functional style)
IEnumerable FibLinq(int n) =>
    Enumerable.Range(0, n)
              .Aggregate(
                  new List { 0, 1 },
                  (acc, _) =>
                  {
                      acc.Add(acc[^1] + acc[^2]);
                      return acc;
                  })
              .Take(n)
              .ToList();

// Method 4: Span for maximum performance (no heap allocation)
void FibSpan(Span buffer)
{
    if (buffer.Length == 0) return;
    buffer[0] = 0;
    if (buffer.Length == 1) return;
    buffer[1] = 1;
    for (int i = 2; i < buffer.Length; i++)
        buffer[i] = buffer[i - 1] + buffer[i - 2];
}

The lazy generator (Method 2) is the most idiomatic C# approach — it computes Fibonacci numbers on demand and can be combined with any LINQ operator. Span<T> (Method 4) is the most performant option when you need to fill a pre-allocated buffer without any heap allocation, which is useful in hot paths. For an interview, demonstrating Method 1 and Method 2 and explaining the trade-offs is an excellent answer.

questions.tip: Recursive Fibonacci is O(2^n) — interview interviewers ask it to see if you know to use memoization/iteration instead. Always show the iterative approach as your preferred solution, then explain memoization as a way to add caching to the recursive version.

questions.question: Find duplicates in an array
questions.answer:
// Method 1: HashSet — O(n) time, O(n) space
IEnumerable<int> FindDuplicates(int[] arr)
{
    var seen = new HashSet<int>();
    var duplicates = new HashSet<int>();
    foreach (var num in arr)
    {
        if (!seen.Add(num))     // Add returns false if already present
            duplicates.Add(num);
    }
    return duplicates;
}

// Method 2: LINQ GroupBy (concise)
IEnumerable<int> FindDuplicatesLinq(int[] arr) =>
    arr.GroupBy(x => x)
       .Where(g => g.Count() > 1)
       .Select(g => g.Key);

// Method 3: Sort + adjacent comparison — O(n log n), O(1) extra space
IEnumerable<int> FindDuplicatesSorted(int[] arr)
{
    Array.Sort(arr);
    var result = new List<int>();
    for (int i = 1; i < arr.Length; i++)
        if (arr[i] == arr[i-1] && (result.Count == 0 || result[^1] != arr[i]))
            result.Add(arr[i]);
    return result;
}

// Variant: find all elements that appear more than once, with count
Dictionary<int, int> CountFrequency(int[] arr) =>
    arr.GroupBy(x => x)
       .ToDictionary(g => g.Key, g => g.Count());

// Example:
// Input:  [1, 3, 4, 2, 2, 3]
// Output: [2, 3]
followUps: What if elements are in range 1..n? (cycle detection)
Find the only duplicate in an array of 1..n

followUpAnswers.What if elements are in range 1..n? (cyc:
When all elements are guaranteed to be in the range 1..n for an array of size n, you can exploit the index-value relationship to detect duplicates without extra memory. The classic technique uses index marking: for each value v, negate arr[v-1]. If it's already negative, then v appeared before.

// Index negation trick — O(n) time, O(1) extra space
// Warning: MODIFIES the input array
List FindDuplicatesInRange(int[] nums)
{
    var result = new List();
    for (int i = 0; i < nums.Length; i++)
    {
        int idx = Math.Abs(nums[i]) - 1;  // map value to index (1-indexed)
        if (nums[idx] < 0)
            result.Add(idx + 1);          // already negated = duplicate
        else
            nums[idx] = -nums[idx];       // mark as visited
    }
    return result;
}
// Input:  [4, 3, 2, 7, 8, 2, 3, 1]
// Output: [2, 3]

// Floyd's cycle detection (for finding ONE duplicate in 1..n)
// Treats the array as a linked list: index → value → next index
int FindDuplicate(int[] nums)  // array size n+1, values in 1..n
{
    // Phase 1: find intersection point in cycle
    int slow = nums[0];
    int fast = nums[0];
    do {
        slow = nums[slow];
        fast = nums[nums[fast]];
    } while (slow != fast);

    // Phase 2: find cycle entrance (= duplicate)
    slow = nums[0];
    while (slow != fast)
    {
        slow = nums[slow];
        fast = nums[fast];
    }
    return slow;
}

Floyd's cycle detection is the most elegant O(n) time O(1) space solution — it models the array as a functional graph where each node i points to nums[i]. Because there's a duplicate, the graph must contain a cycle, and finding the cycle entrance gives the duplicate. This is a popular advanced interview question at FAANG companies.
followUpAnswers.Find the only duplicate in an array of 1:
When an array of size n+1 contains integers from 1 to n with exactly one duplicate, there are several O(1) space solutions, each with different trade-offs: math (sum formula), XOR, binary search on value range, and Floyd's cycle detection.

// Approach 1: Math — sum of 1..n = n*(n+1)/2
// Duplicate = actual sum - expected sum
int FindDuplicateMath(int[] nums)
{
    int n = nums.Length - 1;  // array has n+1 elements
    long expectedSum = (long)n * (n + 1) / 2;
    long actualSum   = nums.Sum(x => (long)x);
    return (int)(actualSum - expectedSum);
}
// Caveat: only works if there is EXACTLY ONE duplicate

// Approach 2: XOR (works even if sum overflows)
int FindDuplicateXOR(int[] nums)
{
    int xor = 0;
    for (int i = 1; i < nums.Length; i++) xor ^= i;      // XOR 1..n
    foreach (int num in nums)                              // XOR all elements
        xor ^= num;
    return xor;  // all pairs cancel; the duplicate remains
}

// Approach 3: Binary search on value range — O(n log n), O(1) space
int FindDuplicateBinarySearch(int[] nums)
{
    int lo = 1, hi = nums.Length - 1;
    while (lo < hi)
    {
        int mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2;
        // Count numbers in range [lo..mid]
        int count = nums.Count(n => n >= lo && n <= mid);
        // By pigeonhole: if count > mid-lo+1, the duplicate is in [lo..mid]
        if (count > mid - lo + 1) hi = mid;
        else lo = mid + 1;
    }
    return lo;
}

// Approach 4: Floyd's cycle detection — O(n), O(1), preserves array
int FindDuplicateFloyd(int[] nums)
{
    int slow = nums[0], fast = nums[0];
    do { slow = nums[slow]; fast = nums[nums[fast]]; }
    while (slow != fast);
    slow = nums[0];
    while (slow != fast) { slow = nums[slow]; fast = nums[fast]; }
    return slow;
}

The math approach is the quickest to explain but fails if there are multiple duplicates. XOR is similar — elegant for exactly-one-duplicate. Binary search on value uses the pigeonhole principle and works even if you cannot modify the array. Floyd's is the most robust: O(n) time, O(1) space, no modifications, handles the constraint perfectly. Lead with XOR or math for simplicity, then present Floyd's as the superior general solution.

questions.tip: If values are in range 1..n: Floyd's cycle detection or XOR trick can solve in O(n) time and O(1) space — but these are advanced. For most interviews, the HashSet approach is expected and optimal enough.

questions.question: Find the two numbers in an array that sum to a target (Two Sum)
questions.answer:
// O(n) — HashMap approach (optimal)
int[] TwoSum(int[] nums, int target)
{
    var seen = new Dictionary<int, int>();  // value → index
    for (int i = 0; i < nums.Length; i++)
    {
        int complement = target - nums[i];
        if (seen.TryGetValue(complement, out int j))
            return [j, i];  // found
        seen[nums[i]] = i;
    }
    throw new ArgumentException("No solution");
}

// O(n log n) — Two pointer (if array is sorted, or if you can sort it)
int[] TwoSumSorted(int[] sortedNums, int target)
{
    int left = 0, right = sortedNums.Length - 1;
    while (left < right)
    {
        int sum = sortedNums[left] + sortedNums[right];
        if (sum == target)  return [left, right];
        if (sum < target)   left++;
        else                right--;
    }
    throw new ArgumentException("No solution");
}

// O(n²) — Brute force (mention but don't use)
int[] TwoSumBrute(int[] nums, int target)
{
    for (int i = 0; i < nums.Length; i++)
        for (int j = i + 1; j < nums.Length; j++)
            if (nums[i] + nums[j] == target)
                return [i, j];
    throw new ArgumentException("No solution");
}

// Example: nums = [2, 7, 11, 15], target = 9 → [0, 1]
followUps: What if there are multiple pairs?
What if the array is sorted?
What about Three Sum?

followUpAnswers.What if there are multiple pairs?:
When the problem allows multiple valid pairs, you need to return all of them rather than short-circuiting on the first match. Adjust the HashMap approach to continue scanning after finding a pair, or group values by frequency first.

// Return ALL pairs that sum to target (indices)
List<(int, int)> TwoSumAllPairs(int[] nums, int target)
{
    var result = new List<(int, int)>();
    var seen = new Dictionary>();  // value → list of indices

    for (int i = 0; i < nums.Length; i++)
    {
        int complement = target - nums[i];
        if (seen.TryGetValue(complement, out var indices))
        {
            foreach (int j in indices)
                result.Add((j, i));
        }
        if (!seen.ContainsKey(nums[i]))
            seen[nums[i]] = new List();
        seen[nums[i]].Add(i);
    }
    return result;
}
// nums = [1, 3, 2, 4, 3, 5], target = 6
// Output: [(1,2) → 3+3, (0,4) → 1+5, (2,3) → 2+4] (varies)

// Return unique VALUE pairs (not index pairs)
IEnumerable<(int a, int b)> TwoSumUniqueValuePairs(int[] nums, int target)
{
    var seen = new HashSet();
    var emitted = new HashSet<(int, int)>();
    foreach (int num in nums)
    {
        int complement = target - num;
        if (seen.Contains(complement))
        {
            var pair = (Math.Min(num, complement), Math.Max(num, complement));
            if (emitted.Add(pair))   // avoid duplicate reporting
                yield return pair;
        }
        seen.Add(num);
    }
}
// nums = [1, 3, 2, 3, 4, 5], target = 6
// Output: (1,5), (2,4), (3,3)

The key distinction is between index pairs (original LeetCode problem, where each element can only be used once) and value pairs (where you want distinct number combinations). Always clarify with the interviewer which variant they want. The emitted HashSet prevents reporting the same pair of values multiple times when duplicates exist in the array.
followUpAnswers.What if the array is sorted?:
A sorted array unlocks the two-pointer technique, which achieves O(n) time with O(1) extra space — better than the HashMap approach's O(n) space. The invariant: if the sum is too small, advance the left pointer; if too large, retreat the right pointer.

// Two-pointer on sorted array — O(n) time, O(1) space
int[]? TwoSumSorted(int[] sorted, int target)
{
    int left = 0, right = sorted.Length - 1;
    while (left < right)
    {
        int sum = sorted[left] + sorted[right];
        if (sum == target) return [left, right];
        if (sum < target)  left++;   // need larger sum
        else               right--;  // need smaller sum
    }
    return null;  // no pair found
}
// [1, 2, 3, 4, 6], target=6 → [1, 3]  (values 2 and 4)

// If the array is NOT sorted but you can sort it (and don't need original indices)
int[]? TwoSumSortFirst(int[] nums, int target)
{
    var sorted = nums.OrderBy(x => x).ToArray();
    int left = 0, right = sorted.Length - 1;
    while (left < right)
    {
        int sum = sorted[left] + sorted[right];
        if (sum == target) return [sorted[left], sorted[right]];
        if (sum < target)  left++;
        else               right--;
    }
    return null;
}
// Note: if you need original indices after sorting, store (value, originalIndex) tuples

// Binary search variant on sorted array — O(n log n)
int[]? TwoSumBinarySearch(int[] sorted, int target)
{
    for (int i = 0; i < sorted.Length - 1; i++)
    {
        int complement = target - sorted[i];
        int idx = Array.BinarySearch(sorted, i + 1, sorted.Length - i - 1, complement);
        if (idx >= 0) return [i, idx];
    }
    return null;
}

The two-pointer approach relies entirely on the sorted property: moving left right only increases the sum, moving right left only decreases it, so every step either finds the answer or definitively eliminates a candidate. This is why O(1) space is achievable — the sort order provides the information that the HashMap was storing. The binary search variant is O(n log n) and typically worse; present it only if the interviewer asks for an alternative to two pointers.
followUpAnswers.What about Three Sum?:
Three Sum asks for all triplets [a, b, c] where a + b + c = 0 (or a given target). The canonical solution sorts the array, then for each element uses the two-pointer technique on the remaining portion — O(n²) time, O(1) extra space (ignoring output).

// Three Sum — O(n²) time, O(1) extra space
IList> ThreeSum(int[] nums)
{
    Array.Sort(nums);
    var result = new List>();

    for (int i = 0; i < nums.Length - 2; i++)
    {
        // Skip duplicates for the first element
        if (i > 0 && nums[i] == nums[i - 1]) continue;

        int left = i + 1, right = nums.Length - 1;
        while (left < right)
        {
            int sum = nums[i] + nums[left] + nums[right];
            if (sum == 0)
            {
                result.Add(new List { nums[i], nums[left], nums[right] });
                // Skip duplicates for the second and third elements
                while (left < right && nums[left]  == nums[left  + 1]) left++;
                while (left < right && nums[right] == nums[right - 1]) right--;
                left++; right--;
            }
            else if (sum < 0) left++;
            else              right--;
        }
    }
    return result;
}

// Example:
// [-4, -1, -1, 0, 1, 2]  →  [[-1,-1,2], [-1,0,1]]

// Four Sum — extend with one more outer loop
// Time: O(n³), pattern: sort + two outer loops + inner two-pointer
IList> FourSum(int[] nums, int target)
{
    Array.Sort(nums);
    var result = new List>();
    for (int i = 0; i < nums.Length - 3; i++)
    {
        if (i > 0 && nums[i] == nums[i-1]) continue;
        for (int j = i+1; j < nums.Length - 2; j++)
        {
            if (j > i+1 && nums[j] == nums[j-1]) continue;
            int left = j+1, right = nums.Length - 1;
            while (left < right)
            {
                long sum = (long)nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[left] + nums[right];
                if (sum == target)
                {
                    result.Add(new List{nums[i],nums[j],nums[left],nums[right]});
                    while (left < right && nums[left]  == nums[left+1])  left++;
                    while (left < right && nums[right] == nums[right-1]) right--;
                    left++; right--;
                }
                else if (sum < target) left++;
                else right--;
            }
        }
    }
    return result;
}

The duplicate-skipping logic (if (nums[i] == nums[i-1]) continue) is the tricky part — it ensures each unique triplet is emitted only once even when the sorted array has repeated values. The general pattern (sort + fix k-2 elements with outer loops + two-pointer on the last two) extends to k-Sum with O(n^(k-1)) time complexity.

questions.tip: Walk through the HashMap approach step by step: for each element, compute what complement you need, check if you've seen it. This one-pass O(n) approach is the canonical answer. The interviewer is testing if you know when to trade space for time.

questions.question: Remove duplicates from a sorted array in-place
questions.answer:
// O(n) time, O(1) space — two-pointer in-place
int RemoveDuplicates(int[] nums)
{
    if (nums.Length == 0) return 0;

    int uniqueCount = 1;  // first element is always unique
    for (int i = 1; i < nums.Length; i++)
    {
        if (nums[i] != nums[i - 1])  // found a new unique value
        {
            nums[uniqueCount] = nums[i];  // place it at next unique position
            uniqueCount++;
        }
    }
    return uniqueCount;  // first uniqueCount elements are the deduplicated array
}

// Example:
// Input:  [1, 1, 2, 3, 3, 4]
// After:  [1, 2, 3, 4, _, _]  (first 4 elements matter)
// Return: 4

// LINQ alternative (creates new array, O(n) space)
int[] RemoveDuplicatesLinq(int[] sorted) =>
    sorted.Distinct().ToArray();  // works on unsorted too (uses HashSet internally)

// For unsorted array with distinct values preserved:
int[] RemoveDuplicatesUnsorted(int[] nums)
{
    var seen = new HashSet<int>();
    return nums.Where(seen.Add).ToArray();  // seen.Add returns false for duplicates
}
followUps: What if the array allows each element at most twice?
What about removing duplicates from an unsorted array?

followUpAnswers.What if the array allows each element at:
This is LeetCode 80 — "Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array II". The approach is the same slow-fast pointer pattern, but you compare the current element with the element two positions back in the deduplicated section (not with the immediately preceding element).

// Allow at most 2 occurrences — O(n) time, O(1) space
int RemoveDuplicatesAtMostTwice(int[] nums)
{
    if (nums.Length <= 2) return nums.Length;

    int k = 2;  // slow pointer; first 2 elements always kept
    for (int i = 2; i < nums.Length; i++)
    {
        // Only accept nums[i] if it differs from the element 2 slots back
        if (nums[i] != nums[k - 2])
        {
            nums[k] = nums[i];
            k++;
        }
    }
    return k;
}

// Trace:
// Input:  [1, 1, 1, 2, 2, 3]
//  i=2: nums[2]=1 == nums[0]=1 → skip
//  i=3: nums[3]=2 != nums[1]=1 → nums[2]=2, k=3
//  i=4: nums[4]=2 != nums[2]=2? NO (nums[k-2]=nums[1]=1, nums[4]=2 != 1 → write)
//       Wait: k=3, k-2=1, nums[1]=1, nums[4]=2 → write, k=4
//  i=5: nums[5]=3 != nums[2]=2 → write, k=5
// Result: [1, 1, 2, 2, 3], k=5  ✓

// Generalised: allow at most K occurrences
int RemoveDuplicatesAtMostK(int[] nums, int k)
{
    if (nums.Length <= k) return nums.Length;
    int writePos = k;
    for (int i = k; i < nums.Length; i++)
    {
        if (nums[i] != nums[writePos - k])
        {
            nums[writePos] = nums[i];
            writePos++;
        }
    }
    return writePos;
}

The generalised version with parameter k elegantly handles "at most once" (k=1, the original problem), "at most twice" (k=2), and any limit. The invariant is: element i is accepted into the output if it is different from the element k positions before the current write position — which ensures no value appears more than k times. This is a beautiful pattern worth memorizing for interview variations.
followUpAnswers.What about removing duplicates from an u:
Without the sorted guarantee you cannot use the adjacent-comparison trick — you need to track which values you've seen. The three approaches are HashSet (O(n) time and space), sorting then deduplicating (O(n log n) time, O(1) extra space), and ordering-preserving in-place via a HashSet write pointer.

// HashSet — O(n) time, O(n) space; preserves order
int[] RemoveDuplicatesUnsorted(int[] nums)
{
    var seen = new HashSet();
    return nums.Where(seen.Add).ToArray();
    // seen.Add(x) returns true the first time, false on subsequent calls
}
// [4, 3, 2, 3, 1, 4, 5] → [4, 3, 2, 1, 5]

// In-place write pointer with HashSet (modifies original array)
int RemoveDuplicatesUnsortedInPlace(int[] nums)
{
    var seen = new HashSet();
    int writePos = 0;
    for (int i = 0; i < nums.Length; i++)
    {
        if (seen.Add(nums[i]))       // true = first occurrence
            nums[writePos++] = nums[i];
    }
    return writePos;  // first writePos elements are the result
}

// Sort-then-deduplicate — O(n log n), O(1) extra space but CHANGES ORDER
int RemoveDuplicatesSortFirst(int[] nums)
{
    if (nums.Length == 0) return 0;
    Array.Sort(nums);
    int k = 1;
    for (int i = 1; i < nums.Length; i++)
        if (nums[i] != nums[i - 1])
            nums[k++] = nums[i];
    return k;
}

// LINQ one-liner (creates new collection)
var distinct = nums.Distinct().ToArray();         // order preserved, HashSet internally
var distinctSorted = nums.Distinct().OrderBy(x => x).ToArray();

The choice depends on constraints: if original order must be preserved, use the HashSet-based write pointer (O(n) space). If order does not matter and memory is tight, sort first and use adjacent comparison (O(1) extra space). For a quick LINQ one-liner in production, nums.Distinct() is perfectly idiomatic. Always clarify with the interviewer whether order preservation is required — it fundamentally changes the algorithm choice.

questions.tip: Slow-fast pointer pattern: slow pointer (uniqueCount) tracks where to write next unique element. Fast pointer (i) scans the array. This O(1) space pattern applies to many array modification problems.

questions.question: Find the maximum subarray sum (Kadane's Algorithm)
questions.answer:
// Kadane's Algorithm — O(n) time, O(1) space
int MaxSubarraySum(int[] nums)
{
    if (nums.Length == 0) throw new ArgumentException("Empty array");

    int maxSum = nums[0];
    int currentSum = nums[0];

    for (int i = 1; i < nums.Length; i++)
    {
        // Either extend current subarray or start fresh from nums[i]
        currentSum = Math.Max(nums[i], currentSum + nums[i]);
        maxSum = Math.Max(maxSum, currentSum);
    }
    return maxSum;
}

// Variant: also return the subarray indices
(int maxSum, int start, int end) MaxSubarrayWithIndices(int[] nums)
{
    int maxSum = nums[0], currentSum = nums[0];
    int maxStart = 0, maxEnd = 0, tempStart = 0;

    for (int i = 1; i < nums.Length; i++)
    {
        if (nums[i] > currentSum + nums[i])
        {
            currentSum = nums[i];
            tempStart = i;
        }
        else
        {
            currentSum += nums[i];
        }
        if (currentSum > maxSum)
        {
            maxSum = currentSum;
            maxStart = tempStart;
            maxEnd = i;
        }
    }
    return (maxSum, maxStart, maxEnd);
}

// Example:
// [-2, 1, -3, 4, -1, 2, 1, -5, 4] → 6 (subarray [4, -1, 2, 1])
followUps: What if the array is all negative?
What is the circular maximum subarray?

followUpAnswers.What if the array is all negative?:
When all elements are negative, the maximum subarray is the single largest element (least negative value). Kadane's algorithm handles this correctly if you initialize both maxSum and currentSum to nums[0] rather than 0. Initializing to 0 would incorrectly return 0 (suggesting an empty subarray), which contradicts the problem if a non-empty subarray is required.

// Correctly handles all-negative arrays
int MaxSubarrayAllNegativeOk(int[] nums)
{
    int maxSum     = nums[0];  // start with first element, NOT 0
    int currentSum = nums[0];
    for (int i = 1; i < nums.Length; i++)
    {
        currentSum = Math.Max(nums[i], currentSum + nums[i]);
        maxSum     = Math.Max(maxSum, currentSum);
    }
    return maxSum;
}

// All-negative examples:
// [-5, -3, -1, -4] → -1   (the least-negative value)
// [-7, -2, -9]     → -2
// [-1]             → -1

// WRONG: initializing to 0 breaks all-negative case
int MaxSubarrayWrong(int[] nums)
{
    int maxSum = 0;      // bug: suggests empty subarray is valid
    int currentSum = 0;
    foreach (int n in nums)
    {
        currentSum = Math.Max(n, currentSum + n);
        maxSum = Math.Max(maxSum, currentSum);
    }
    return maxSum;  // returns 0 for [-3, -1, -2] — incorrect
}

// Variant: if the problem explicitly allows empty subarray (sum = 0)
// then initializing maxSum = 0 is correct and intentional

This is a deliberate trap in many interviews — the candidate is expected to discuss the initialization choice. Always ask the interviewer: "Can the answer be an empty subarray?" If yes, initialize to 0. If a non-empty subarray is required, initialize to nums[0] and handle the empty-input case with a guard clause. Mentioning this ambiguity proactively signals strong problem-solving communication skills.
followUpAnswers.What is the circular maximum subarray?:
The Maximum Circular Subarray Sum (LeetCode 918) extends Kadane's algorithm to arrays that "wrap around" — the subarray can include the end and beginning of the array. There are two possible cases: either the optimal subarray does NOT wrap (standard Kadane's answer) or it DOES wrap (total sum minus the minimum non-wrapping subarray).

// Maximum subarray sum in a circular array
int MaxCircularSubarraySum(int[] nums)
{
    // Case 1: max subarray does NOT wrap → standard Kadane's
    int maxSum = KadaneMax(nums);

    // Case 2: max subarray WRAPS → totalSum - minSubarraySum
    // Removing the minimum subarray from the middle leaves the wrap-around portion
    int totalSum = nums.Sum();
    int minSum   = KadaneMin(nums);
    int circularMax = totalSum - minSum;

    // Edge case: if all elements are negative, circularMax = 0 (empty array)
    // but we need a non-empty subarray, so return maxSum in that case
    if (circularMax == 0) return maxSum;  // all elements negative

    return Math.Max(maxSum, circularMax);
}

// Standard Kadane (maximum)
int KadaneMax(int[] nums)
{
    int maxSum = nums[0], curr = nums[0];
    for (int i = 1; i < nums.Length; i++)
    {
        curr   = Math.Max(nums[i], curr + nums[i]);
        maxSum = Math.Max(maxSum, curr);
    }
    return maxSum;
}

// Kadane for minimum subarray
int KadaneMin(int[] nums)
{
    int minSum = nums[0], curr = nums[0];
    for (int i = 1; i < nums.Length; i++)
    {
        curr   = Math.Min(nums[i], curr + nums[i]);
        minSum = Math.Min(minSum, curr);
    }
    return minSum;
}

// Examples:
// [1, -2, 3, -2]       → 3  (no wrap: [3])
// [5, -3, 5]           → 10 (wrap: [5, 5] across boundary)
// [-3, -2, -3]         → -2 (all negative: single max element)

The elegance of this solution is that the wrap-around case is equivalent to taking the total sum and subtracting the minimum contiguous subarray (the portion we don't want). Both cases are O(n) and can be computed in a single pass by tracking four running values simultaneously: maxEndingHere, maxSoFar, minEndingHere, and minSoFar. This is a well-known extension question that demonstrates deep understanding of Kadane's core insight.

questions.tip: Kadane's key insight: at each position, you decide to either extend the previous subarray or start a new one from the current element. Whichever is larger becomes the current sum. Keep track of the global maximum.

questions.question: Implement a stack using a queue and a queue using a stack
questions.answer:
// Stack using two queues — push O(n), pop O(1)
public class StackUsingQueues<T>
{
    private Queue<T> _q1 = new();
    private Queue<T> _q2 = new();

    public void Push(T item)
    {
        _q2.Enqueue(item);               // enqueue to empty q2
        while (_q1.Count > 0)           // move all q1 to q2
            _q2.Enqueue(_q1.Dequeue());
        (_q1, _q2) = (_q2, _q1);        // swap references (q1 is now the stack)
    }

    public T Pop()  => _q1.Dequeue();
    public T Peek() => _q1.Peek();
    public bool IsEmpty => _q1.Count == 0;
}

// Queue using two stacks — enqueue O(1), dequeue amortized O(1)
public class QueueUsingStacks<T>
{
    private Stack<T> _inbox  = new();   // new items go here
    private Stack<T> _outbox = new();   // items dequeued from here

    public void Enqueue(T item) => _inbox.Push(item);

    public T Dequeue()
    {
        if (_outbox.Count == 0)           // outbox empty: refill from inbox
        {
            while (_inbox.Count > 0)
                _outbox.Push(_inbox.Pop()); // reverses order = FIFO
        }
        return _outbox.Pop();
    }

    public T Peek()
    {
        if (_outbox.Count == 0)
            while (_inbox.Count > 0)
                _outbox.Push(_inbox.Pop());
        return _outbox.Peek();
    }
}
followUps: What is the amortized complexity of QueueUsingStacks.Dequeue()?
What is the Min Stack problem?

followUpAnswers.What is the amortized complexity of Queu:
The amortized complexity of Dequeue() in the two-stack queue is O(1) amortized, even though a single call can take O(n) time (when the outbox is empty and must be refilled from the inbox).

The proof uses the accounting / potential method: assign each element a "credit" of 2 operations when it is pushed onto the inbox (Enqueue). One credit pays for the Push itself; the second credit is saved. When the element is later moved from inbox to outbox, that second credit pays for the transfer Pop + Push. The final Pop from outbox is "free" because both stacks are O(1). Since every element is moved at most once, the total work across N operations is O(N), giving O(1) per operation on average.

// Trace demonstrating amortized O(1):
// Enqueue(1)  → inbox: [1]       outbox: []
// Enqueue(2)  → inbox: [1,2]     outbox: []
// Enqueue(3)  → inbox: [1,2,3]   outbox: []
// Dequeue()   → outbox empty! Move all: outbox: [3,2,1]  ← O(3) work here
//              → pop from outbox → returns 1
// Dequeue()   → outbox: [3,2] → pop → returns 2          ← O(1) work
// Dequeue()   → outbox: [3]   → pop → returns 3          ← O(1) work
// Enqueue(4)  → inbox: [4]       outbox: []
// Dequeue()   → outbox empty! Move 1 item: outbox: [4]   ← O(1) work
//              → returns 4

// Total 7 operations: 3 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 7 units of work → O(1) amortized

// Worst case per call: O(n) (when refilling outbox)
// Worst case total for N operations: O(N) → O(1) amortized

The key insight: each element traverses from inbox to outbox exactly once. This one-time migration cost is "charged" at enqueue time, so Dequeue never pays twice for the same element. This is the standard amortized analysis that interviewers at senior/staff level expect you to articulate clearly.
followUpAnswers.What is the Min Stack problem?:
The Min Stack problem (LeetCode 155) asks you to implement a stack that supports push, pop, top, and getMin() — all in O(1) time. The trick is to maintain a parallel auxiliary stack that tracks the current minimum at each level of the main stack.

public class MinStack
{
    private readonly Stack _stack    = new();
    private readonly Stack _minStack = new();  // tracks minimum at each level

    public void Push(int val)
    {
        _stack.Push(val);
        // Push to minStack: the new minimum is the smaller of val and current min
        int currentMin = _minStack.Count == 0 ? val : Math.Min(val, _minStack.Peek());
        _minStack.Push(currentMin);
    }

    public void Pop()
    {
        _stack.Pop();
        _minStack.Pop();  // keep the two stacks in sync
    }

    public int Top()    => _stack.Peek();
    public int GetMin() => _minStack.Peek();  // O(1)!
}

// Trace:
// Push(-2) → stack:[-2]       minStack:[-2]
// Push(0)  → stack:[-2,0]     minStack:[-2,-2]
// Push(-3) → stack:[-2,0,-3]  minStack:[-2,-2,-3]
// GetMin() → -3  ✓
// Pop()    → stack:[-2,0]     minStack:[-2,-2]
// Top()    → 0   ✓
// GetMin() → -2  ✓

// Space-optimised variant: store only when a new minimum is set
public class MinStackOptimised
{
    private readonly Stack _stack    = new();
    private readonly Stack _minStack = new();

    public void Push(int val)
    {
        _stack.Push(val);
        if (_minStack.Count == 0 || val <= _minStack.Peek())
            _minStack.Push(val);  // only push when val is a new minimum
    }

    public void Pop()
    {
        int top = _stack.Pop();
        if (top == _minStack.Peek())  // if we're removing the current minimum
            _minStack.Pop();
    }

    public int Top()    => _stack.Peek();
    public int GetMin() => _minStack.Peek();
}

The first approach uses O(n) auxiliary space guaranteed. The optimised version uses less space in the average case (minStack only grows when a new minimum is found), but worst case is still O(n). Both are O(1) for all operations. The <= comparison (not just <) in the optimised version is critical: if you push the same minimum value multiple times, you need it in minStack multiple times so that popping one copy doesn't lose track of the remaining copies.

questions.tip: Amortized O(1) for dequeue: each element crosses from inbox to outbox at most once. Even though a single dequeue might take O(n) to refill, the average across N operations is O(1). This is the amortized analysis explanation interviewers want.

questions.question: Implement binary search
questions.answer:
// Iterative — O(log n) time, O(1) space (preferred)
int BinarySearch(int[] sortedArr, int target)
{
    int left = 0, right = sortedArr.Length - 1;
    while (left <= right)
    {
        int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;  // avoid integer overflow vs (left+right)/2
        if (sortedArr[mid] == target) return mid;
        if (sortedArr[mid] < target)  left = mid + 1;
        else                           right = mid - 1;
    }
    return -1;  // not found
}

// Recursive
int BinarySearchRecursive(int[] arr, int target, int left, int right)
{
    if (left > right) return -1;
    int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
    if (arr[mid] == target) return mid;
    if (arr[mid] < target)  return BinarySearchRecursive(arr, target, mid + 1, right);
    else                     return BinarySearchRecursive(arr, target, left, mid - 1);
}

// Find first occurrence of target (lower bound)
int FirstOccurrence(int[] sorted, int target)
{
    int left = 0, right = sorted.Length - 1, result = -1;
    while (left <= right)
    {
        int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
        if (sorted[mid] == target) { result = mid; right = mid - 1; }  // continue left
        else if (sorted[mid] < target) left = mid + 1;
        else right = mid - 1;
    }
    return result;
}

// .NET built-in: Array.BinarySearch(sortedArray, target)
// Returns index if found, ~index of next larger element if not found
followUps: What is the time complexity?
How do you search in a rotated sorted array?

followUpAnswers.What is the time complexity?:
Binary search runs in O(log n) time and O(1) space for the iterative version. Each iteration halves the search space, so the number of iterations is at most ⌊log₂(n)⌋ + 1.

Array Size (n)Max Iterations (log₂ n)
164
1,00010
1,000,00020
1,000,000,00030
// Why O(log n)? Recurrence relation:
// T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1)
// Applying Master Theorem (case 2): T(n) = O(log n)

// Space complexity:
// Iterative: O(1) — only 3 integer variables (left, right, mid)
// Recursive: O(log n) — call stack depth = log n frames

// Proof of correctness for mid overflow fix:
int left = int.MaxValue - 1;  // 2,147,483,646
int right = int.MaxValue;     // 2,147,483,647
// (left + right) / 2 = integer overflow → -1 (wrong!)
// left + (right - left) / 2 = 2,147,483,646 + 1/2 = 2,147,483,646 ✓

// Best case: O(1) — target is at the midpoint immediately
// Worst case: O(log n) — target is at a leaf of the binary search tree
// Average case: O(log n)

// Comparison with linear search:
// n = 1,000,000: linear search = up to 1,000,000 ops
//                binary search = up to 20 ops
//                That's a 50,000x speedup!

The recursive version is O(log n) time but O(log n) space due to the call stack — each recursive call is not tail-recursive in C# (the final operation is a conditional return, not a simple tail call). The iterative version is preferred in production for this reason. When pressed on the O(log n) derivation, citing the Master Theorem and the halving recurrence T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) is the rigorous answer.
followUpAnswers.How do you search in a rotated sorted ar:
A rotated sorted array is one that was sorted, then shifted at some pivot: e.g., [4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 1, 2]. Normal binary search fails because the array is no longer uniformly sorted. The key insight: after splitting at mid, at least one half is always sorted — and you can check which half to search based on whether the target falls within that sorted half.

// Search in rotated sorted array — O(log n)
int SearchRotated(int[] nums, int target)
{
    int left = 0, right = nums.Length - 1;
    while (left <= right)
    {
        int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
        if (nums[mid] == target) return mid;

        // Determine which half is sorted
        if (nums[left] <= nums[mid])  // left half is sorted
        {
            // Is target in the sorted left half?
            if (nums[left] <= target && target < nums[mid])
                right = mid - 1;  // search left
            else
                left = mid + 1;   // search right
        }
        else  // right half is sorted
        {
            // Is target in the sorted right half?
            if (nums[mid] < target && target <= nums[right])
                left = mid + 1;   // search right
            else
                right = mid - 1;  // search left
        }
    }
    return -1;
}

// Examples:
// [4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 1, 2], target=0 → index 4
// [4, 5, 6, 7, 0, 1, 2], target=3 → -1
// [1], target=0 → -1

// Extension: find the pivot index (minimum element)
int FindMinInRotated(int[] nums)
{
    int left = 0, right = nums.Length - 1;
    while (left < right)
    {
        int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
        if (nums[mid] > nums[right])
            left = mid + 1;   // min is in right half
        else
            right = mid;      // min is in left half (inclusive of mid)
    }
    return left;  // index of minimum
}

// With duplicates (harder variant — worst case degrades to O(n))
int SearchRotatedWithDuplicates(int[] nums, int target)
{
    int left = 0, right = nums.Length - 1;
    while (left <= right)
    {
        int mid = left + (right - left) / 2;
        if (nums[mid] == target) return mid;
        // Can't determine sorted half when duplicates exist
        if (nums[left] == nums[mid] && nums[mid] == nums[right])
        { left++; right--; continue; }  // shrink both sides
        if (nums[left] <= nums[mid])
        {
            if (nums[left] <= target && target < nums[mid]) right = mid - 1;
            else left = mid + 1;
        }
        else
        {
            if (nums[mid] < target && target <= nums[right]) left = mid + 1;
            else right = mid - 1;
        }
    }
    return -1;
}

The critical observation is nums[left] <= nums[mid]: if this is true, the left portion [left..mid] has no rotation and is fully sorted. You can then do a simple range check to decide which side to search. The duplicate case is trickier — when all three of nums[left], nums[mid], and nums[right] are equal, you cannot determine which side is sorted, so you must shrink both boundaries, degrading worst case to O(n).

questions.tip: mid = left + (right - left) / 2 — not (left + right) / 2. The latter can overflow if left and right are both large ints. This is a classic bug that crashed the Java standard library binary search implementation in 2006. Always mention it.
